description: Rewrite eye_detection.js with multi-signal proctoring: browser focus detection (tab switch/window blur), mouse leaving browser, WebGazer.js for actual eye/gaze tracking, plus head pose as supplementary backup. This detects subtle eye movements without requiring head movement.
steps: Add WebGazer.js CDN script tag to templates/exam_questions.html before eye_detection.js

Completely rewrite static/js/eye_detection.js with multi-signal approach: browser focus events (immediate warning on tab switch/blur), mouse leaving window (2s delay), WebGazer.js gaze tracking (2s delay when gaze outside exam area), head pose as supplementary (2s delay)
Keep same UI overlays and warning count logic (MAX_WARNINGS=5, auto-hide after 5s)
acceptance_criteria: Tab switch / window blur triggers IMMEDIATE warning (no delay)
Mouse leaving browser window triggers warning after 2 seconds
WebGazer.js gaze outside exam area triggers warning after 2 seconds
Head pose detection triggers warning after 2 seconds (backup signal)
Each signal independently can trigger a warning
One warning per distraction event, reset when back to normal
findings: Implemented multi-signal proctoring combining 4 independent detection signals. WebGazer.js CDN added for ML-based eye tracking that detects subtle gaze shifts without head movement. Browser focus events (visibilitychange, blur) provide immediate detection of tab/window switching. Mouse leave adds a physical cursor boundary signal. Head pose from face-api.js remains as a backup. All 37 backend tests pass. The key improvement is that WebGazer.js uses machine learning to predict actual gaze coordinates on screen, enabling detection of eye-only movements that the previous head-pose-only approach could not detect.
